The **preserved counties of Wales** ([Welsh](/wiki/Welsh_language "Welsh language"): *siroedd cadwedig*) are the eight current areas used in [Wales](/wiki/Wales "Wales") for the ceremonial purposes of [lieutenancy](/wiki/Lord-lieutenant "Lord-lieutenant") and [shrievalty](/wiki/High_Sheriff#England,_Wales_and_Northern_Ireland "High Sheriff"). They are based on the [counties](/wiki/Subdivisions_of_Wales "Subdivisions of Wales") which were used for local government and other purposes between 1974 and 1996. Each comprises one or more of the 22 single-tier [principal areas](/wiki/Principal_areas_of_Wales "Principal areas of Wales") which are used for administrative purposes.

## Usage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Preserved_counties_of_Wales&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Usage")]

The [Local Government (Wales) Act 1994](/wiki/Local_Government_(Wales)_Act_1994 "Local Government (Wales) Act 1994") abolished the eight ceremonial counties created by the [Local Government Act 1972](/wiki/Local_Government_Act_1972 "Local Government Act 1972"). However, it created the concept of preserved counties based on their areas, to be used for purposes such as [lieutenancy](/wiki/Lord-lieutenant "Lord-lieutenant"). This usage was [consolidated](/wiki/Consolidation_bill "Consolidation bill") by the [Lieutenancies Act 1997](/wiki/Lieutenancies_Act_1997 "Lieutenancies Act 1997").[[1]](#cite_note-1) Certain statutes already in force were amended to include reference to them.

## Boundary changes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Preserved_counties_of_Wales&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Boundary changes")]

The preserved counties were originally almost identical to the 1974–96 counties, but with a few minor changes in line with local government boundary changes: [Llanrhaeadr-ym-Mochnant](/wiki/Llanrhaeadr-ym-Mochnant "Llanrhaeadr-ym-Mochnant"), [Llansilin](/wiki/Llansilin "Llansilin") and [Llangedwyn](/wiki/Llangedwyn "Llangedwyn") were transferred from [Clwyd](/wiki/Clwyd "Clwyd") to [Powys](/wiki/Powys "Powys"), and [Wick](/wiki/Wick,_Glamorgan "Wick, Glamorgan"), [St Brides Major](/wiki/St_Brides_Major "St Brides Major"), [Ewenny](/wiki/Ewenny "Ewenny") and [Pentyrch](/wiki/Pentyrch "Pentyrch") were transferred from [Mid Glamorgan](/wiki/Mid_Glamorgan "Mid Glamorgan") to [South Glamorgan](/wiki/South_Glamorgan "South Glamorgan"). There were however two local government areas, [Caerphilly](/wiki/Caerphilly_County_Borough "Caerphilly County Borough") and [Conwy](/wiki/Conwy_County_Borough "Conwy County Borough"), split between preserved counties.

The Local Government Boundary Commission for Wales were instructed by the [National Assembly for Wales](/wiki/Senedd_Cymru_%E2%80%93_Welsh_Parliament "Senedd Cymru – Welsh Parliament") on 11 March 2002 to undertake a review of preserved county boundaries. In their final proposals the part of the local government area of [Caerphilly](/wiki/Caerphilly_County_Borough "Caerphilly County Borough") which had been in Mid Glamorgan was to be part of Gwent and the part of the local government area of [Conwy](/wiki/Conwy_County_Borough "Conwy County Borough") which had been in [Gwynedd](/wiki/Gwynedd_(preserved_county) "Gwynedd (preserved county)") was to be part of [Clwyd](/wiki/Clwyd "Clwyd"). The boundary between Mid Glamorgan and South Glamorgan was also to be re-aligned to reflect small changes in local government boundaries.[[2]](#cite_note-lgbc-2) The Assembly accepted these proposals such that from 2 April 2003 each preserved county encompassed between one and five whole local government areas.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

The boundary between West Glamorgan and Powys was further modified on 1 April 2005 as a result of boundary changes between Ystalyfera and Ystradgynlais.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

The boundary between Mid Glamorgan and Powys was further modified on 1 April 2010 to reflect the 2009 local government boundary changes in the area around [Vaynor](/wiki/Vaynor "Vaynor"), [Merthyr Tydfil](/wiki/Merthyr_Tydfil_County_Borough "Merthyr Tydfil County Borough").[[5]](#cite_note-5)
